title: Designing a field service tool people would actually use
subtitle: Sanitised
context: A field service company with 36 technicians across northern and central Italy. Intervention requests come in through a call center or directly via CRM — then a 6-person back office team handles planning and assignment.
problem: The CRM couldn't support the complexity of their operations. The back office had no reliable way to track technician availability, skills, or notes — so they defaulted to spreadsheets. At the same time, the company needed to maximize the number of interventions per day, which required smarter planning logic that the existing tools couldn't provide.
role: I mapped the full workflow — from incoming request to technician assignment — through conversational interviews with both back office staff and technicians. From there I defined the information architecture, designed the interfaces, and worked in parallel with the developer throughout implementation.
process:
The key challenge was translating the freedom of a spreadsheet into a structured interface without losing flexibility. I identified the core decisions the back office made daily — who is available, who has the right skill, who is closest — and designed around those, rather than around the CRM's existing logic.

Two complexities emerged during the project. The first was territory logic: rather than dividing Italy into fixed zones, we resolved it through action radiuses — each technician has a range, and consecutive interventions are planned within it. This kept planning flexible and realistic.

The second was intervention types: work orders come from the CRM, but cases are created manually by the back office during phone calls and only become work orders later. The planning view had to hold both states simultaneously without creating confusion.

The agenda itself follows a spreadsheet-like logic — no fixed time slots, free placement — with a map view showing pending interventions geographically. This preserved the flexibility the team was used to, while adding the structure they were missing.
outcome: The platform is currently going into production. During development, the back office team used it directly for testing, and the interface was refined through multiple rounds of real use. No metrics yet — but the tool replaced a spreadsheet that had been running their operations for years.

title: Restructuring a courier management system mid-growth
subtitle: Sanitised
context: A Milan-based bike courier service operating from two warehouses. A back office team of dispatchers creates routes and assigns them to couriers. Couriers use a lightweight app to manage deliveries in real time — confirming drops, flagging issues.
problem:
The platform was originally built around a single model: multi-stop routes with pickups and deliveries. Over time, a second type was introduced — queue-based deliveries, point-to-point from supermarket to end customer. The system hadn't been designed for both, and the distinction wasn't reflected in the interface.

At the same time, dispatchers were struggling with a gap in the original logic: there was no clear way to track when the same package needed both a pickup and a delivery. The design system had also fallen behind — when queues appeared on the live map, there were no defined colors or visual rules to distinguish them.
role: I joined an existing project mid-development, inheriting a partially defined interface and an incomplete design system. I audited what existed, identified the gaps, and took ownership of the missing pieces — redesigning the views that weren't working, defining the design system rules, and working in parallel with the developer throughout.
process:
The core work was bringing order to a system that had grown faster than its design. The first step was establishing a visual language: routes and queues were assigned distinct colors on the live map, and separated into two tabs in the planning view — making the operational difference between the two models explicit in the interface.

The route cards were the most redesigned element. They became expandable — accordion-style — so dispatchers could get a quick overview without losing access to detail. The cards were redesigned to surface more information in less space, and delivery statuses were systematized to be consistent and readable across states.

The missing design system rules were defined in parallel: color logic, component states, status vocabulary — building the foundation that would let the platform scale without accumulating more inconsistency.
outcome: The platform is in use. The pickup/delivery ambiguity — one of the dispatchers' main pain points — was resolved through chips and consistent naming conventions, making the state of each package unambiguous at a glance. The design system, previously undefined, now gives the team a shared language to build on.

title: Giving a research center a digital presence that reflects its work
subtitle: Codici Ricerche e Intervento
context: Codici is a Milan-based social cooperative working at the intersection of research and social intervention. Their work is project-based, editorially rich, and driven by a clear methodology — but their website didn't reflect any of it.
problem:
The site had a flat structure that flattened everything into a few generic sections. Projects existed but their outputs were buried. Events and news were mixed together with no distinction. And the annual research grant — one of the most distinctive things Codici does, a public call that funds two projects each year — had no dedicated presence at all.

The result was a site that listed what they did without communicating how or why they did it.
role: I led the full project — analysing the existing site and content, redefining the information architecture, designing wireframes and UI, and managing the relationship with the client through multiple rounds of iteration. Branding was rebuilt from scratch, keeping only the existing logo as a fixed point.
process:
The central decision was architectural: everything on the site revolves around projects. Events, products, calls for collaborators, instant updates — all become children of a project, linked from its page rather than existing as standalone sections. This wasn't just a structural choice; it reflected how Codici actually works.

Each project page became a diary — a chronological record of phases, outputs, and connections. Nothing is buried: if something happened within a project, it lives there.

News as a category was abolished. Content with an editorial angle goes into Codici404, the cooperative's journal. Everything else becomes an update in the project diary. The result is a site with no orphaned content and no generic catch-all sections.

The annual research grant — previously invisible — became a first-level section: an archive of calls, funded projects, and outcomes. Making it visible was one of the clearest ways to communicate how Codici thinks, not just what it does.
outcome: The site is not yet live, but the project is complete. The client was initially resistant to some of the more radical decisions — abolishing news as a category, restructuring everything around projects — but came around through the iteration process. The final result is a site built around how Codici actually works, not around what a website is supposed to look like.

title: Building a tool to teach visual misinformation
subtitle: Density Design
context: A university project developed at Density Design in 2019, when fact-checking labels on social media didn't exist yet. The brief asked to design a digital artifact that would invite a specific audience to reflect and act on a researched topic. The team addressed people who used social media as their primary news source — without the tools or habits to question what they were seeing.
problem:
Visual misinformation is not one thing. A manipulated image can be a photomontage, a mislabeled photo, or a real image stripped of its context — each mechanism works differently, but all exploit the same assumption: that seeing means knowing. Most people who encounter false images online aren't careless, they're simply unequipped.

The research behind the project mapped this complexity across real debunked cases, showing how frequently images are falsified around major events — and how rarely the distinction between types of manipulation is made visible.
role: Within the team I contributed to the research phase, led the UX and interaction design, and wrote part of the code. The project moved from research findings to concept to working artifact — I was involved across all three.
process:
The quiz format emerged as the right choice because it replicates the actual experience: you see an image, you make a judgment, you find out you were wrong. The interaction is designed to create a moment of surprise — not to shame the user, but to make the mechanism of manipulation viscerally clear.

Each question presents two or four images — or a single image with manipulated details — and asks the user to identify the false one without context. The reveal comes immediately after: not just the answer, but the specific type of manipulation at play. Photomontage, mislabeling, decontextualisation — each has a different logic, and the explanation makes that visible.

The interface simulated the visual language of social media and news sources, making the cases feel familiar and immediate rather than academic.
outcome: The project was picked up by Tactical Tech, who translated it into 22 languages and distributed it internationally. Built in 2019, before fact-checking labels existed on social platforms, DoubleCheck was ahead of a conversation that became mainstream shortly after.

title: Visualizing what your smart objects know about you
subtitle: Master's thesis
context: A self-initiated master's thesis in Communication Design, developed independently from concept to code. The project addresses a problem that affects everyone but that few people can actually explain: what data do smart objects collect, how is it processed, and what can it reveal about you.
problem:
Smart objects are ubiquitous but opaque. People interact with them daily without understanding the data layer underneath — not because they don't care, but because the system is genuinely complex. Data flows through first, second, and third parties; different devices capture different types of information; and what can be inferred from that data goes far beyond what is explicitly collected.

The challenge was making this complexity navigable without simplifying it into something false.
role: I defined the research question, mapped the system, designed the full experience, and oversaw development — working with collaborators on the React implementation while remaining responsible for every interaction and narrative decision.
process:
The core design challenge was representing a system that is both complex and deliberately opaque. The solution was a network visualization that maps each interaction — turning up the thermostat, switching on a light — to the data it generates, and then traces how that data moves through first, second, and third parties.

Dashed lines represent uncertainty: data that may or may not reach a party, depending on agreements the user has no visibility into. The blur follows the same logic — making the unknowability of the system a visual property, not just a footnote.

Each device is explored separately, keeping the experience focused. The emphasis is not just on what data is collected, but on what can be inferred from it — presence in the house, room usage patterns, daily habits. The gap between raw data and what it reveals is where the real privacy risk lives, and the project makes that gap visible.
outcome: Graduated with 110L — the commission was struck both by the topic and the execution. But the most deliberate design decision was how the project ends: not with alarm, but with agency. After mapping what companies know, the experience offers concrete guidance on how to use each device more safely. The goal was never to frighten — it was to equip.

title: Art directing a report on the Italian design sector
subtitle: Fondazione Symbola, POLI.design
context: An annual report commissioned by Fondazione Symbola and POLI.design mapping the economic and cultural state of the Italian design sector — structured datasets, survey results, and editorial content across a publication-length document.
role: I was responsible for art direction, layout, and data visualization. The work involved defining the visual language of the report, designing the charts and infographics, and laying out the full document.
process: The main challenge was maintaining visual coherence across a large volume of heterogeneous content — data-heavy spreads alongside editorial text, charts of different types and scales. Each visualization required decisions about the right form for the data and how to integrate it with the surrounding layout without losing either analytical clarity or editorial quality.
outcome: The report was published and distributed by Fondazione Symbola. It is available online at symbola.net.

title: Mapping complex geographies for an editorial series
subtitle: Iperborea
context: The Passenger is Iperborea's travel and culture series — each issue dedicated to a country or region, combining journalism, essays, and visual content into a single editorial object.
role: I contributed cartographic and data visualization work for multiple issues, designing maps and visual systems to support the editorial content.
process: Each map required translating complex geographic and cultural datasets into a form that could work within the constraints of print — fixed dimensions, limited color, and readers who are not data specialists. The work involved both the visual design and the underlying decisions about what to show and how to frame it.
outcome: The visualizations were published across multiple issues of The Passenger series by Iperborea.

title: Building the visual system for a data-driven portrait of Milan
subtitle: Propp
context: A data-driven project exploring Milan through cartographic visualizations that go beyond its institutional narratives — using data and geography to surface less visible urban dynamics.
role: Working within Propp, I translated structured datasets into maps and visual systems designed to support interpretation rather than direct it.
process: Each visualization required decisions about what to show, what to omit, and how much framing to apply. The goal was to let the data surface patterns without over-determining how a reader should respond to them — leaving room for exploration rather than imposing a single reading.
outcome: The project was completed and published by Propp.

title: Visualizing Antarctica as a geopolitical and environmental system
subtitle: The Pomo
context: An editorial research project treating Antarctica as a geopolitical and environmental system, using data visualization to layer and communicate complex, multi-source information.
role: As part of a data visualization team at The Pomo, I translated research datasets into publication-ready visuals — using RAWGraphs for exploration and Illustrator for refinement.
process: Working within strict editorial constraints required close attention to consistency, accuracy, and visual integration. Each chart had to hold up analytically while fitting within a tightly defined visual system shared across the whole publication.
outcome: The project was published by The Pomo and is available in open access at antarctic-resolution.org.

title: Working through data visualization across studies and early commissions
context: A collection of data visualization work produced during Communication Design studies at Politecnico di Milano and through early freelance commissions — different formats, scales, and contexts.
role: Each piece involved the full process: data collection and cleaning, visual exploration, and final design. Tools varied by project — RAWGraphs, D3, Illustrator, Figma.
process: The projects span editorial infographics, interactive experiments, and print-ready charts. Working across different briefs and constraints was how I developed a consistent approach to translating data into visual form — learning what works by trying many things.

title: Designing and building a research lab's first digital presence
subtitle: Politecnico di Milano
description: Branding, UX design, and WordPress development for the Circular Food System Lab at Politecnico di Milano. The site was conceived as a scalable framework, designed and prototyped in Figma and built to support future growth in content and research outputs.

title: Building a flexible identity for an evolving research journal
subtitle: DiscoJournal
description: Branding, web design, and WordPress development for a research collective publishing annual issues on shifting themes. The work focused on defining a visual and structural system flexible enough to evolve across editions while preserving coherence and usability.

title: Branding and building a website for an Italian amaro
subtitle: Vero Amaro Favero
description: Branding, web design, and development for Vero Amaro Favero, a small Italian amaro producer. The project covered visual identity and the design and build of the website.
